title: Classification of Systems
summary: This page explains the superposition principle for linear systems, showing that if an input is expressed as a sum of simpler functions, the output is the corresponding sum of individual responses. It illustrates how arbitrary inputs can be approximated by sums of impulses or step functions, enabling system analysis through known impulse or step responses. The page also previews frequency-domain methods using sinusoids and exponentials covered in later chapters.

figures.caption: Signal representation in terms of impulse and step components.
figures.description: The figure has two side-by-side plots of signal amplitude x(t) versus time t, each showing a smooth rising curve and a marked sampling interval Δt on the time axis. In panel (a), the curve is approximated by a sequence of narrow vertical rectangular slices, illustrating representation as weighted impulse-like components over successive time intervals. In panel (b), the same curve is built from stacked horizontal step-like rectangles, illustrating representation in terms of step components. Students should notice that both constructions approximate the same continuous signal as Δt becomes small.
